__data__.text: Single Cells Exhibit Differing Behavioral Phases during Early Stages of Pseudomonas aeruginosa Swarming Chinedu S. Madukoma, a Peixian Liang, b Aleksandar Dimkovikj, a Jianxu Chen, b Shaun W. Lee, c Danny Z. Chen, b Joshua D. Shrout a,c aDepartment of Civil and Environmental Engineering and Earth Sciences, University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, Indiana, USA bDepartment of Computer Science and Engineering, University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, Indiana, USA

ABSTRACT Pseudomonas aeruginosa is among the many bacteria that swarm, where groups of cells coordinate to move over surfaces. It has been challenging to determine the behavior of single cells within these high-cell-density swarms. To track individual cells withinP. aeruginosa swarms, we imaged a ﬂuorescently labeled subset of the larger population. Single cells at the advancing swarm edge varied in their motility dynamics as a function of time. From these data, we delineated four phases of early swarming prior to the formation of the tendril fractals characteristic of P. aeruginosa swarming by collectively considering both micro- and macroscale data. We determined that the period of greatest single-cell motility does not coin- cide with the period of greatest collective swarm expansion. We also noted that ﬂagellar, rhamnolipid, and type IV pilus motility mutants exhibit substantially less single-cell motility than the wild type. IMPORTANCE Numerous bacteria exhibit coordinated swarming motion over sur- faces.

IMPORTANCE Numerous bacteria exhibit coordinated swarming motion over sur- faces. It is often challenging to assess the behavior of single cells within swarming communities due to the limitations of identifying, tracking, and analyzing the traits of swarming cells over time. Here, we show that the behavior of Pseu- domonas aeruginosa swarming cells can vary substantially in the earliest phases of swarming. This is important to establish that dynamic behaviors should not be assumed to be constant over long periods when predicting and simulating the ac- tions of swarming bacteria. KEYWORDS Pseudomonas aeruginosa, collective motion, mean square displacement, swarming, tracking any surface-motile bacteria, such asPseudomonas aeruginosa, Proteus mirabilis, Bacillus subtilis, Salmonella enterica, Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Escherichia coli, and Sinorhizobium meliloti, exhibit collective motion known as “swarming” (1–6).

While all swarming occurs at generally high cell densities, the speciﬁc population density of swarms can vary spatially and temporally, as dynamic clusters of bacteria constantly change by combining and separating within the swarm ( 7–9). Additionally, not all swarm phenotypes are equal; temperate swarming bacteria such asP. aeruginosa do not develop typical swarmer cell phenotypes, such as the elongated hyperﬂagellated cells that characterize swarming of other bacteria. The conditions that promote swarm- ing also vary among species ( The expansion of bacterial swarms requires sufﬁcient liquid to establish a thin ﬁlm on colonized surfaces that provides for both bacterial growth and ﬂagellar motion. The motility of individual motile cells within the thin liquid ﬁlm generates ﬂow lines that drive the swarm outward from the point of inoculation. This phenomenon has been well characterized forB.

This phenomenon has been well characterized forB. subtilis swarming, where stationary cells tend to be oriented in Citation Madukoma CS, Liang P, Dimkovikj A, Chen J, Lee SW, Chen DZ, Shrout JD. 2019. Received 8 March 2019 Accepted 9 July 2019 Accepted manuscript posted online 15 July Published RESEARCH ARTICLE crossm October 2019 Volume 201 Issue 19 e00184-19 jb.asm.org 1Journal of Bacteriology 6 September 2019 the direction of the swarm expansion following the ﬂow lines positioned by the swarm, while motile cells can orient perpendicular to the ﬂow, enabling them to travel independently between ﬂow lines ( 11). In order to understand the individual and collective behaviors of swarming cells, methods such as single-particle tracking, real- time computer tracking, and combined three-dimensional (3-D) tracking ( have been used. Such methods are useful for examining phenotypic behavior of mutants and/or the effect of various nutrients ( 18–23). Certainly, one or more quanti- tative measures are needed to speciﬁcally delineate physiochemical aspects that control bacterial swarming and collective motion at the population level.

While the transition and differentiation of swarmer cells to or from planktonic or bioﬁlm cells have been characterized for many bacterial species ( 5, 24, 25), the variations exhibited by single cells within the swarming population are only marginally understood. Swarming cells ofB. subtilis and Serratia marcesens exhibit superdiffusive trajectories, consistent with the Lévy walk, where cells travel in continually changing straight lines ( 26). The clustering of single cells within larger swarms also inﬂuences the collective behavior of the group (7, 19). Darnton et al. (27) noted greater differences in thedistributionofcellspeedfor E. coliawayfromtheswarmedgeandwithinmultilayer plateaus compared to cells at the advancing swarm edge. However, these ﬁndings and other prior work generally characterize that the velocity and other traits of swarming bacteria do not change temporally.

However, these ﬁndings and other prior work generally characterize that the velocity and other traits of swarming bacteria do not change temporally. Mathematical simulations of bacterial swarming have generally assumed constant velocities for individual cells ( 9, 28, 29) or have computed cell velocities within a deﬁned range rather than actual measured velocities 11, 30). Improvement upon this prior work has been limited by a lack of spatial and temporal understanding of single-cell behavior for most swarming bacteria. In this study, we examined regions of swarming P. aeruginosa closest to the advancing swarm edge. We detailed the behavior of the single cells within these regions to understand the collective movement of expanding swarms over time. Little has been characterized about the behavior of singleP. aeruginosa cells during swarm- ing compared to other swarming bacteria, and some differences would be expected between polar mono-ﬂagellatedP. aeruginosa compared to multiﬂagellatedB.

aeruginosa compared to multiﬂagellatedB. subtilis, E. coli, P. mirabilis, and others. Also, we examined two time and spatial scales that are relevant to assessingP. aeruginosa swarming. Lastly, we tracked single-cell behavior of threecommonlystudiedtypesofswarmmutants,strainsdeﬁcientforapolarﬂagellum, type IV pili, and rhamnolipid. By tracking individual cells within the swarm edge, we quantiﬁed velocity, displacement, and other attributes and compared these data to the collective expansion behavior. From these results, we deﬁned four phases of early swarming for P. aeruginosa and noted several attributes of tracked cells within and between these four phases.
